skill.name: nextjs-app-router-patterns
skill.description: Master Next.js 14+ App Router with Server Components, streaming, parallel routes, and advanced data fetching patterns.
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for building modern Next.js applications with App Router architecture and Server Components.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains documentation-only markdown files with Next.js educational code examples. All 56 static findings are false positives because the scanner incorrectly flagged markdown code blocks as executable code. No actual security risks detected.

content.user_title: Build Modern Next.js Apps with App Router Patterns
content.value_statement: Struggling with Next.js 14+ App Router complexity? This skill provides proven patterns for Server Components, streaming, parallel routes, and data fetching to accelerate your development.
actual_capabilities: Generate Server Component patterns with proper data fetching strategies
Implement streaming UI with Suspense boundaries for progressive loading
Design parallel and intercepting routes for complex layouts and modals
Create Server Actions for form handling and mutations
Configure caching strategies including ISR and tag-based invalidation
Set up metadata generation for SEO and social sharing
limitations: Does not execute or test generated code - manual verification required
Examples assume Next.js 14+ with App Router only, not Pages Router
Does not replace official Next.js documentation for edge cases
Code examples require adaptation to your specific project structure

use_cases.title: Migrate from Pages Router to App Router
use_cases.description: Step-by-step guidance for transitioning existing Next.js applications to the new App Router architecture with minimal disruption.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers maintaining legacy Next.js codebases

use_cases.title: Build Performance-Optimized E-commerce Sites
use_cases.description: Implement streaming product pages, parallel cart updates, and ISR caching for fast, scalable online stores.
use_cases.target_user: E-commerce developers building high-traffic shopping experiences

use_cases.title: Create Dashboard Applications with Real-time Data
use_cases.description: Design complex layouts with independently loading panels using parallel routes and Suspense boundaries.
use_cases.target_user: SaaS developers building analytics and admin dashboards

prompt_templates.title: Basic Server Component Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Next.js Server Component that fetches products from an API and displays them in a grid. Include proper error handling and loading states.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with Server Components for data fetching

prompt_templates.title: Implement Streaming with Suspense
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a product detail page that streams reviews and recommendations progressively. Use Suspense boundaries to show content as it loads.
prompt_templates.scenario: Improving perceived performance for pages with multiple data sources

prompt_templates.title: Create Server Actions for Form Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a checkout form using Server Actions with validation, error handling, and redirect on success. Include optimistic UI updates.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Parallel Routes for Dashboard
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a dashboard layout with independent analytics, team, and activity panels that load in parallel.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complex applications requiring multiple independent data loading paths

output_examples.input: Create a Server Component that fetches blog posts with ISR caching
output: Generated app/blog/page.tsx with async data fetching
Configured revalidate: 3600 for hourly cache refresh
Added loading.tsx skeleton for better UX during revalidation
Included error boundary for graceful failure handling

output_examples.input: Implement a modal that intercepts photo routes
output: Set up default.tsx fallback for direct navigation
Implemented modal component with proper close behavior

best_practices: Start with Server Components by default and add 'use client' only when browser APIs or interactivity are required
Place data fetching close to where the data is consumed for optimal caching and code organization
Use Suspense boundaries strategically to enable streaming and improve perceived performance
anti_patterns: Using useState or useEffect in Server Components where they cannot execute
Fetching all data at the top level instead of colocating fetches with consuming components
Ignoring loading states which results in poor user experience during data fetches

faq.question: What is the difference between Server Components and Client Components?
faq.answer: Server Components render on the server and send HTML to the browser. They can access backend resources directly. Client Components run in the browser and support interactivity with useState, useEffect, and event handlers.

faq.question: How do I migrate from Pages Router to App Router?
faq.answer: Create an app/ directory alongside your pages/ directory. Gradually migrate routes by recreating them in app/. Both routers can coexist during migration. Update imports and adapt to the new file conventions.

faq.question: When should I use Server Actions versus API routes?
faq.answer: Use Server Actions for form submissions and mutations triggered from components. Use API routes when you need a REST endpoint for external consumers or non-React clients.

faq.question: How does caching work in App Router?
faq.answer: App Router caches fetch requests by default. Use cache: 'no-store' to disable caching, cache: 'force-cache' for static caching, or next: { revalidate: N } for ISR. Tag-based invalidation allows targeted cache updates.

faq.question: Can I use parallel routes with dynamic segments?
faq.answer: Yes, parallel routes work with dynamic segments. Each parallel slot receives the same params. Use the slot naming convention like @analytics and @team in your layout to define independent rendering areas.

faq.question: What are intercepting routes used for?
faq.answer: Intercepting routes allow you to show different UI for the same URL based on navigation context. Common use cases include modals that appear when navigating from within the app but show full pages on direct visits.
